3.2 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables

distribution_analysis.least_covered_section: 3.1 Revision on Linear Inequalities in one Variable

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 3.1 Revision on Linear Inequalities in one Variable
questions.question: Given that \(a > b\) and \(c < 0\), which statement is always true?
questions.hint: Adding the same number to both sides preserves the inequality.
questions.explanation: Adding \(c\) to both sides: \(a + c > b + c\) (Option C) is always true regardless of \(c\)'s sign. Multiplying by a negative \(c\) reverses the sign, so \(ac < bc\) (not \(>\)), making Option A false.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.1 Revision on Linear Inequalities in one Variable
source_trace.content_anchor: If a > b, a + c > b + c

explanations_tiered.definition: The addition property of inequalities states that adding the same number to both sides preserves the inequality direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Adding any real number \(c\) to both sides of \(a > b\) yields \(a + c > b + c\). This holds for all \(c\), positive or negative.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Adding the same number to both sides never flips the sign, even if the number is negative.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(a > b \Rightarrow a + c > b + c\) for all real \(c\). Multiplication by negative \(c\) flips the sign, but addition does not.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The addition property is a direct consequence of the ordered field axioms for real numbers.
misconceptions: Students often think adding a negative number reverses the sign, but it does not.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Add/subtract: sign same. Multiply/divide by negative: sign flips.

glossary_entry.definition: If a > b then a + c > b + c for any real c.

flashcard.front: True or false: If a > b and c < 0, then a + c > b + c.
flashcard.back: True. Addition never reverses the inequality sign.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: If a > b, does adding c preserve the inequality?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Yes, a + c > b + c for any real c.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Adding the same number to both sides preserves the inequality.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with multiplication by negative numbers.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Addition always preserves the inequality regardless of the sign of c.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 3.2 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables
questions.question: To graph \(y < 2x - 4\), a student tests the point \((0,0)\) and gets \(0 < -4\) (false). Which region should be shaded?
questions.hint: If the test point makes the inequality false, shade the opposite side.
questions.explanation: The test point method: if the test point satisfies the inequality, shade its side; if false, shade the opposite side. Since \((0,0)\) gives \(0 < -4\) (false), shade the half-plane that does NOT contain \((0,0)\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.2 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables
source_trace.content_anchor: If the inequality is false for the test point, shade the opposite region

explanations_tiered.definition: The test point method determines shading by checking a single point not on the boundary line.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If the test point satisfies the inequality, shade its side; if false, shade the opposite side. Here, (0,0) fails, so shade opposite.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pick a point like (0,0). If it works, color its side. If not, color the other side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Testing (0,0) in y < 2x - 4 gives 0 < -4, false. Shade the half-plane opposite (0,0).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality defines a half-plane. The test point (0,0) lies in the false region, so the solution is the complementary half-plane.
misconceptions: Some students shade the test point's side when it fails.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Test point true → shade its side. False → opposite side.

glossary_entry.definition: A technique to determine the solution half-plane by testing a point not on the boundary.

flashcard.front: Test point (0,0) in y < 2x - 4 gives false. Where to shade?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Test point false: shade which side?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Method to determine shading side for linear inequalities.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Always test (0,0) unless it lies on the boundary.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. False test point means shade opposite side.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(|3x - 5| > 1\). Which interval notation represents the solution?
questions.hint: |k| > c means k < -c or k > c. Solve each inequality separately.
questions.explanation: \(3x - 5 < -1\) or \(3x - 5 > 1\). First: \(3x < 4\) → \(x < \frac{4}{3}\). Second: \(3x > 6\) → \(x > 2\). Solution: \((-\infty, \frac{4}{3}) \cup (2, \infty)\). Option B is correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: |k| > c means k < -c or k > c

explanations_tiered.definition: The inequality |k| > c with c > 0 means k is more than c units from zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Solve two inequalities: 3x-5 < -1 gives x < 4/3; 3x-5 > 1 gives x > 2. The solution is the union.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Split into two cases: 3x-5 < -1 or 3x-5 > 1. Solve each, then combine with 'or'.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solution set is the complement of the closed interval [4/3, 2] on the real line.
misconceptions: Some students solve as -1 < 3x-5 < 1, which is the rule for <.

glossary_entry.definition: |k| > c with c>0 is equivalent to k < -c or k > c.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. 3x-5 < -1 or 3x-5 > 1 → x < 4/3 or x > 2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
questions.question: Solve \(x^2 - 2x - 15 \leq 0\). What is the solution set in interval notation?
questions.hint: Factor the quadratic and use the product property for ≤ 0.
questions.explanation: Factor: \((x-5)(x+3) \leq 0\). Critical points: -3 and 5. The product is ≤ 0 when one factor is ≤ 0 and the other ≥ 0, which occurs between -3 and 5 inclusive. Solution: \([-3, 5]\).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties

explanations_tiered.definition: A quadratic inequality ≤ 0 is satisfied between the roots when the parabola opens upward.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Factor to (x-5)(x+3) ≤ 0. The product is negative or zero between the roots -3 and 5, inclusive.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find roots: -3 and 5. The parabola opens up, so it's ≤ 0 between the roots.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: (x-5)(x+3) ≤ 0 → -3 ≤ x ≤ 5, since between the roots one factor is non-positive, the other non-negative.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solution is the closed interval [-3,5], where the quadratic expression is non-positive.
misconceptions: Students often choose the outside intervals instead of between the roots.

glossary_entry.definition: Inequality where quadratic expression is ≤ 0, typically between roots if leading coefficient positive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A family's monthly spending on food (f) and education (e) and recreation (r) is at most Birr 5,000. Which inequality models this?
questions.hint: "At most" means less than or equal to.
questions.explanation: "At most Birr 5,000" means the total spending cannot exceed 5,000, so \(f + e + r \leq 5000\). Option C is correct. Options A and D indicate exceeding or meeting at least; Option B is strict inequality without the boundary.

explanations_tiered.definition: "At most" translates to the inequality symbol ≤ (less than or equal to).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Total spending = f + e + r. "At most 5000" means total ≤ 5000.
explanations_tiered.beginner: At most means the total can be 5000 or less, so use ≤.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: f + e + r ≤ 5000 is the correct inequality for a maximum budget.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The constraint defines a closed half-space in ℝ³, bounded by the plane f+e+r=5000.
misconceptions: Students confuse at most (≤) with at least (≥).

glossary_entry.definition: Less than or equal to, indicating a maximum allowable value.

flashcard.front: At most 5000 means what inequality symbol?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: At most Birr 5000 for f+e+r. Inequality?

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 3.1 Revision on Linear Inequalities in one Variable
questions.question: Solve \(4 - 3x > 10\). What is the solution set?
questions.hint: Subtract 4 from both sides, then divide by -3, remembering to flip the sign.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.1 Revision on Linear Inequalities in one Variable

explanations_tiered.definition: Solving linear inequalities involves isolating the variable using inverse operations while respecting sign-flip rules.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Subtract 4: -3x > 6. Divide by -3 (negative): flip > to <, giving x < -2.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Move 4 to the other side: -3x > 6. Divide by -3: when dividing by negative, flip the sign → x < -2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solution is the open interval (-∞, -2).
worked_solution: Step 3: Divide by -3, flip sign: x < -2
reasoning_steps: Divide by coefficient, flip sign if negative
misconceptions: Students often forget to flip the inequality sign when dividing by a negative number.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind about sign flip when dividing by negative.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 3.2 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables
questions.question: A student graphs \(y > 2x + 1\) with a solid line. What error did the student make?

options.A: The line should be dashed because \(>\) is strict
options.D: The shading should be below the line

questions.hint: Strict inequalities (< or >) use dashed boundary lines.
questions.explanation: The inequality is \(y > 2x + 1\), which is strict (no equality). The boundary line should be dashed. A solid line is used for ≤ or ≥. Option A correctly identifies the error.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.2 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables
source_trace.content_anchor: If symbol is < or >, line is dashed

explanations_tiered.definition: Strict inequalities (< or >) use dashed boundary lines because points on the line are not included.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The student used a solid line for y > 2x+1. Since > is strict, the correct line is dashed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When the inequality has < or > (no equal sign), the line should be dashed, not solid.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For y > 2x+1, points on the line y = 2x+1 do not satisfy the inequality, so the line must be dashed.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Solid lines indicate inclusion of the boundary, used for ≤ or ≥. Strict inequalities exclude the boundary.
misconceptions: Students often use solid lines for all inequalities, forgetting the rule.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: > or < → dashed line. ≥ or ≤ → solid line.

glossary_entry.definition: A dashed line used when the inequality does not include equality.

flashcard.front: What type of line for y > 2x+1?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Using solid line for strict inequality
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Strict inequality > requires dashed line.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
questions.question: A sign chart shows that \((x+2)(x-4)\) is positive on \((-\infty, -2)\) and \((4, \infty)\), zero at -2 and 4, and negative on \((-2, 4)\). Which inequality has this solution pattern?
questions.hint: Positive on the outside intervals means > 0.
questions.explanation: The sign chart shows positivity on \((-\infty, -2)\) and \((4, \infty)\), which is the pattern for > 0. Option B matches. Option A would be negative on those intervals; Options C and D include the endpoints differently.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
source_trace.content_anchor: Sign chart displays sign of factors

explanations_tiered.definition: A sign chart shows where an expression is positive, negative, or zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Positive on outside intervals and negative between means the expression is > 0 on those intervals.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Positive on the ends means the expression is greater than zero there, so the inequality is > 0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The sign pattern matches (x+2)(x-4) > 0, which is positive outside the roots.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solution set (-∞,-2) ∪ (4,∞) corresponds to the inequality (x+2)(x-4) > 0.
reasoning_steps: Identify where sign chart shows positive
misconceptions: Some students match the sign pattern to the opposite inequality.

glossary_entry.definition: Positive intervals indicate > 0, negative intervals indicate < 0.

flashcard.front: Positive on ends, negative in middle. What inequality?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Sign: + on ends, - in middle. Inequality?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A table showing sign of expression on intervals
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Positive on ends means > 0.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Dendir works x hours at Birr 10/hour and y hours at Birr 15/hour. He needs at least Birr 240. If he works 10 hours at the Birr 15/hour job, how many hours must he work at the Birr 10/hour job?
questions.hint: Substitute y = 10 into \(10x + 15y \geq 240\) and solve for x.
questions.explanation: Inequality: \(10x + 15(10) \geq 240\) → \(10x + 150 \geq 240\) → \(10x \geq 90\) → \(x \geq 9\). He needs at least 9 hours at the Birr 10/hour job.

explanations_tiered.definition: Substitute known values into an inequality to solve for the remaining variable.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The minimum x is 9 hours to meet the earnings target when tutoring for 10 hours.
misconceptions: Students may subtract incorrectly or forget to isolate x properly.

glossary_entry.definition: Replacing a variable with a known value to solve for another.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Inequality modeling income from two jobs

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(|x - 2| < 0\). What is the solution?
questions.hint: Absolute value is always ≥ 0. Can it be less than 0?
questions.explanation: Absolute value is always non-negative. \(|x-2| < 0\) would require a negative absolute value, which is impossible. Thus, there is no solution.

explanations_tiered.definition: An absolute value inequality of the form |expression| < 0 has no solution because absolute value is always ≥ 0.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since |x-2| ≥ 0 for all real x, it can never be less than 0. Therefore, no solution.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Absolute value can't be negative, so |x-2| < 0 is impossible. No x works.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The range of |x-2| is [0, ∞). There is no x for which |x-2| < 0.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality |x-2| < 0 has an empty solution set because the absolute value function's minimum is 0.
misconceptions: Some students solve as -0 < x-2 < 0, getting x=2 incorrectly.

glossary_entry.definition: An absolute value inequality that cannot be satisfied because the right-hand side is negative for < or ≤.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 3 — Absolute Value Special Cases
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: When absolute value is less than a negative number
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that absolute value is always ≥ 0.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. |x-2| ≥ 0 for all x, so cannot be < 0.
